region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for canned sardine in oil (drained); SNOMED CT coverage for processed fish products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned sardine in oil (drained) is approximately 90 g, equivalent to the drained contents of one small standard can, providing about 187 kcal, 22 g protein, and 10 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Canned Sardine in Oil (Drained) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for canned sardine in oil (drained), CR ID F03040022. Saturated fat and fatty acid sub-fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 (Sardine, Atlantic, canned in oil, drained solids with bone) for completeness of fatty acid profile, as HPB does not report fatty acid sub-fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: N/A — Canned sardine in oil (drained) contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Canned sardine in oil (drained) contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; no GI value exists or is meaningful. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI entry for this food category. Per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy numeric field requirements, gi_category is set to not_applicable, and the gi_source documents that GI is not applicable for this food type.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 307 mg/100 g (borderline range) offset by high potassium at 397 mg/100 g; favourable K:Na ratio supports DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension may consume drained sardines in moderation (up to 90 g per serving, 2–3 times per week), avoiding additional salty condiments such as soy sauce or fish sauce; rinsing briefly under water after draining can further reduce sodium by an estimated 10–15%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (24.6 g/100 g) and omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) improve insulin sensitivity and reduce metabolic inflammation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned sardine in oil (drained) is glycaemically neutral with 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; pair with high-fibre vegetables and limit to 2–3 servings per week given cholesterol content of 142 mg/100 g in those with diabetic dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA, ~4.4 g PUFA/100 g) which reduce triglycerides, lower cardiovascular inflammation, and support endothelial function; saturated fat is low at ~2.8 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Drained sardines are a cardioprotective food recommended by the American Heart Association (2 servings of oily fish per week); the high omega-3 content reduces triglycerides and cardiovascular event risk, though individuals with established hypercholesterolaemia should monitor total dietary cholesterol given 142 mg/100 g in this food.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) suppress TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; calcium at 382 mg/100 g from soft bones supports bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Drained sardines are among the richest dietary sources of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and provide 382 mg calcium per 100 g from edible soft bones, making them particularly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients; those on RA medications affecting lipid metabolism should monitor total dietary cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned sardine in oil (drained) refers to small, oily marine fish of the genus Sardina or Sardinops that have been commercially canned in vegetable or olive oil and then drained of the packing oil before consumption or nutritional analysis. The draining process removes a significant portion of the added oil, reducing total fat compared to undrained sardines while retaining the fish's intrinsic omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA). The fish are typically packed whole or as fillets, often with soft edible bones intact, which contributes to the exceptionally high calcium content of 382 mg per 100 g — a level comparable to dairy products. The texture is soft and flaky with a rich, savoury, slightly briny flavour. Nutritionally, drained sardines are a powerhouse: 24.6 g protein per 100 g, 11.45 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, and 208 kcal per 100 g. Sodium is 307 mg per 100 g after draining — borderline but offset by high potassium at 397 mg per 100 g, supporting a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is elevated at 142 mg per 100 g, warranting moderation in individuals with dyslipidaemia. Canned sardines are widely consumed across Singapore, Southeast Asia, Europe, and globally as a convenient, affordable, and nutritionally dense protein source. They are used in toast toppings, rice dishes, pasta, salads, and as a standalone protein. Clinically, they are recommended for cardiovascular health, Type 2 Diabetes management (glycaemically neutral), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory omega-3 profile). The glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates.
common_user_queries: How much protein in canned sardine?
Is sardine good for blood pressure?
Is drained sardine lower in sodium?
Can I eat sardine if I have high cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F03040022) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid sub-fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 (Sardine, Atlantic, canned in oil, drained solids with bone), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this food type.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040022); sardines contain no digestible carbohydrates — naturally zero. Sugar_g is naturally zero as sardines contain no sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived food product containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 cross-reference. Saturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.84 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (4.41 g/100 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 (Atlantic sardine, canned in oil, drained solids with bone) as HPB does not report fatty acid sub-fractions; this is documented as an adapted cross-reference. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin and mineral data beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this specific entry (F03040022); USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 was consulted for cross-reference but values were not mapped to avoid mixing source provenance for micronutrients not confirmed by the primary source. The input description was incorrect (described pork ham rather than sardine) and has been corrected to accurately describe canned sardine in oil (drained). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); per Veritas numeric field requirements, glycemic_index is set to the numeric value 0 as a sentinel for not-applicable zero-carbohydrate foods, gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, animal product), sodium_mg (307.0), saturated_fat_g (2.8 — cross-referenced USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088), sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero) all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric value 0 (sentinel for not-applicable zero-carbohydrate foods) satisfying the numeric field requirement raised in iteration 2 validator feedback. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database and explains the not-applicable status. Protocol correctly applied.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for sardine (canned). FoodEx2 code A00LC with description for sardines canned in oil drained. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The iteration 2 failure on q4_glycemic_completeness (glycemic_index must be numeric) has been resolved by setting glycemic_index to the numeric sentinel value 0 with gi_category not_applicable and full documentation in gi_source and notes. The input had a critical description error (pork ham description on a sardine record) which was corrected. All mandatory multilingual names for the Singaporean food context are present. Core macros are complete with zero values justified as naturally zero. Ontology is honest with defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with comprehensive nutritional and clinical content. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
